title: Global News: Politics, Tech, and a Tortoise
summary: This episode covers global political tensions, a major train crash trial, robotaxi troubles, Italy's football heartbreak, and remembers the world's oldest tortoise.
script:
Hello everyone, and welcome to 'Global Insights,' your quick look at the week's most important stories. I'm your host, and today we have a packed show. We'll be talking about big political moves, a sad story from a courtroom, some tech troubles, a sports disappointment, and we'll remember a very special animal. Let's dive right in.

First up, we have some important news from the United States. President Trump is facing a lot of pressure right now. Many people in his home country want him to avoid a very long war. This kind of war, called a 'protracted conflict,' means a fight that goes on for a long time. No one wants a war that drags on and on. So, President Trump is going to give a special speech. This speech will be in the evening, during 'primetime,' when many people are watching TV. He will talk to the country about the war. Everyone is waiting to hear what he will say next and what his plans are to keep the conflict from becoming too long and difficult.

While we're on the topic of President Trump, there are also new questions about his comments on NATO. NATO is a big group of countries that work together for defense. Trump has criticized some NATO members. He says they haven't shown enough support for the United States' goals, especially when it comes to Iran. His comments have made people wonder what his plans are for NATO in the future. Will he decide to leave this alliance? What would that mean for how countries work together around the world? These are big questions that many people are thinking about right now.

Now, let's move to a different part of the world, to the Middle East. We've seen news that Israel is increasing its attacks on Lebanon. 'Intensifies attacks' means they are hitting more places or hitting them harder. These attacks are now reaching areas that are not usually controlled by Hezbollah. Hezbollah is a powerful political party and military group in Lebanon. Israel has also said that it plans to control large parts of south Lebanon. This is a developing situation, and it means there's a lot of tension and fighting in that region.

Moving on to Europe, we have a story from Hungary. There's a new political challenge for Viktor Orban. Mr. Orban has been the leader of Hungary for a long time. He first won elections in 2010 and has won four times in a row since then. But now, a man named Peter Magyar is becoming a big threat to Orban's power. Magyar used to be an ally, or friend, of Orban. But now he is trying to win power himself. This is the biggest challenge Orban has faced in Hungary in many years. It will be interesting to see how this political fight plays out.

Still in Europe, but with a very sad story from Greece. Many families have filled a Greek courtroom. They are there for the trial about the deadliest train crash in Greece's history. This terrible accident happened in 2023. Fifty-seven people lost their lives when two trains crashed into each other. It was a very tragic day for Greece. Now, 36 people have been accused in this case. The trial has just begun, and it is expected to last for years. This shows how complicated and long it is to find out what really happened and to bring justice for such a huge disaster.

Let's switch gears and talk about technology, or rather, a technology problem. In a Chinese city, there was a big issue with 'robotaxis.' These are self-driving cars that work like taxis. They drive themselves without a person in control. But in this city, there was a 'mass malfunction.' This means many of them stopped working properly all at once. This problem halted traffic, meaning it stopped cars from moving. At least 100 robotaxis were affected. The company that makes these robotaxis, called Baidu, has not said anything about what happened. This incident makes us think about how much we rely on new technology and what happens when it goes wrong.

From tech to sports, we have some disappointing news for football fans in Italy. The Italian national football team, also known as the Azzurri, has failed to qualify for the World Cup again. This is the third World Cup in a row that they will not be playing in. The World Cup is one of the biggest and most exciting football tournaments in the world. For a country like Italy, which has a very rich football history and passionate fans, this is a huge blow. Fans and the team's management are all wondering what went wrong. It's a sad time for Italian football lovers.

And finally, on a different note, we say goodbye to a very special creature. Jonathan, the world's oldest known tortoise, has died. He was an amazing 193 years old! That's right, 193 years old. His vet, the doctor who took care of him, said Jonathan left behind a 'legacy of resilience and longevity.' 'Resilience' means being able to recover quickly from difficulties, and 'longevity' means living for a very long time. Jonathan truly showed us how long life can be and how tough some creatures are. He lived through many, many changes in the world. He was a true marvel and will be remembered for his incredible long life.

And that's all the time we have for 'Global Insights' today. Thank you for tuning in and staying informed with us. We'll be back next time with more news from around the world. Until then, take care!
